common_name: Early Girl Tomato (Classic Early-Season Slicing Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Early Girl is named for its ability to produce ripe tomatoes weeks earlier than standard-season varieties.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Early Girl is one of the most popular home garden tomato varieties in American history — a determinate-to-indeterminate hybrid introduced by Petoseed in 1975 that produces medium-sized (4–6 oz), globe-shaped, deep-red slicing tomatoes 50–60 days from transplant, weeks before most main-season varieties ripen. The combination of early maturity, classic tomato flavor, reliable production, and adaptability to a wide range of climates made Early Girl the best-selling tomato variety in many US markets for decades. In zone 9, Early Girl performs best in the spring window (February–June transplanting), producing the first ripe tomatoes well ahead of main-season varieties while temperatures are still moderate. The plant is a compact to medium-sized indeterminate that requires staking but is less vigorous than large-vined cherry tomatoes — a cage of 48–60 inches is usually sufficient. The classic red, round, full-flavored fruits are excellent for fresh slicing, sandwiches, and summer salads where a moderately sized, well-flavored tomato is preferred over giant beefsteak types. Good disease resistance package makes it dependable in humid Gulf South conditions.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun 2–4 weeks after the last frost date when soil is reliably warm; in zone 9, jalapeños often overwinter as perennials and produce a second season's harvest.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the soil has warmed thoroughly; Jalapeño is the most widely grown and most universally recognized hot pepper in American gardens and cuisine — a medium-heat (2,500–8,000 Scoville units) pepper with thick-walled, cylindrical to conical, dark-green fruits that turn red when fully mature. The glossy green jalapeños are harvested at 2.5–3.5 inches long before turning red for the classic fresh, pickled, and canned jalapeño used in Mexican, Tex-Mex, and American cooking. Red-ripe jalapeños are sweeter and somewhat hotter than green-harvested fruit. In zone 9, jalapeños thrive through the warm season and often survive mild winters as perennials, re-sprouting from the base to produce a large, established second-year plant with significantly higher fruit production than first-year plants. The compact, bushy habit requires no staking and makes jalapeños appropriate for container culture as well as garden beds. Harvest green jalapeños when the fruit is firm and full-sized; allow some fruits to turn red for a sweeter product. Excellent for fresh salsa, pickling, stuffing (chile poppers/jalapeños stuffed with cream cheese), and smoking (chipotles are dried, smoked jalapeños).
sources: University of New Mexico Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun after soil is thoroughly warm; serrano peppers are native to the highlands of Mexico and thrive in zone 9's long hot season.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the soil has warmed; Serrano is the second most popular fresh-use hot pepper in Mexican and American cuisine after jalapeño — producing small (1.5–2.5 inch), slender, cylindrical, thick-walled fruits in green that turn red when fully ripe, with significantly more heat than jalapeños (10,000–25,000 Scoville units) and a crisp, clean, bright pepper flavor that makes them outstanding for fresh salsas, guacamole, and uncooked preparations where jalapeños would be too mild. Unlike jalapeños, serranos are almost always used fresh rather than pickled, and their thin walls make them unsuitable for stuffing but excellent for blending into sauces and salsas where their intense heat and fresh pepper flavor blend seamlessly. The compact, productive plants set fruit in large numbers — a single established serrano plant can produce 50–100 or more fruits through the season. In zone 9, serrano peppers perform exceptionally well through the long hot season and often survive mild winters as perennials, developing into large, woody-stemmed plants in their second year. The plants produce fruit prolifically in the Gulf South's long warm season and are among the most heat-tolerant of all common garden pepper cultivars.
sources: University of New Mexico Cooperative Extension
New Mexico State University Cooperative Extension

common_name: Kentucky Wonder Bean (Old Homestead Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 60°F; provide a trellis, pole, or fence 5–8 feet tall for the climbing vines; in zone 9, sow February–April for spring crop and again August–September for fall crop.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun after last frost when soil is warm; Kentucky Wonder is the most famous and most enduring American heirloom pole bean — an heirloom variety in continuous cultivation since the 1860s that remains one of the most widely grown and best-regarded pole beans in American home gardens. The vigorous climbing vines reach 5–8 feet and require a sturdy support structure — traditional pole, trellis, or fence. Kentucky Wonder produces heavy yields of long (7–9 inch), round-to-flat, stringless-when-young green pods with exceptional flavor — a rich, full, classic green bean taste that is considerably more complex and satisfying than modern bush beans bred for commercial production. Seed can be saved true-to-type from open-pollinated Kentucky Wonder. The dry seeds (beans) can be used as shell beans or dried for winter storage, giving the plant dual utility as both green snap bean and dry bean. In zone 9, the spring season (February–May) and fall season (August–October) provide two productive growing windows. The combination of outstanding flavor, prolific production, historical significance, and heirloom status makes Kentucky Wonder the gold standard of American pole beans.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 70°F, or start transplants indoors 3 weeks early; provide a trellis for vertical growing to maximize air circulation and ease of harvest; in zone 9, sow March–April for spring and again August for fall.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; Diva is an All-America Selections Award winner (2002) and one of the most acclaimed cucumbers introduced in the past quarter century — a parthenocarpic (self-fruitful, seedless) cucumber that produces extremely uniform, smooth-skinned, 6–8 inch fruits with thin, tender, never-bitter skin that requires no peeling and no bitterness removal. Unlike most cucumbers that require cross-pollination by insects for reliable fruit set, Diva is parthenocarpic — each flower sets fruit without pollination, ensuring consistent production regardless of pollinator activity. The fruit is reliably seedless or nearly so, with crisp, mild, sweet flesh and completely absent the bitterness that affects some cucumbers. Diva's thin skin, which requires no peeling, and its mild, sweet, never-bitter flavor make it outstanding for fresh eating, salads, and children's snacking. The vigorous, prolific vines produce heavily over a long season — harvest fruits at 6–8 inches before seeds begin to develop for the best eating quality. In zone 9, Diva performs well in the spring and fall growing windows and is among the most heat-tolerant, productive cucumbers for the Gulf South.
